narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 40, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many peers would envy. Your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 7 also place you in the upper half of your classes, ahead of averages around 5.5-5.7. Coming from an upper-middle-class family background further positions you favorably compared to many others in the applicant pool. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 43% chance you'll fail the bar on your first attempt—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's bar passage rates. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your plan to attend part-time, a choice only about 7% of students make compared to the overwhelming 93% who go full-time; the model sees this as a major risk because part-time schedules have historically correlated with lower bar success in similar cases. On a positive note, your Year 3 decile rank worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing by showing recent academic strength. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a solid boost toward the "pass" side, signaling consistent performance that the model values highly. While your other strengths like LSAT and family income helped balance things, the part-time commitment outweighed them as the top influence, tipping the scales just enough for rejection. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive—it's just how this predictive tool weighs patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan, as that alone could flip the model's outlook dramatically based on these patterns. You might also boost your profile by retaking the LSAT for an even higher score or gathering letters emphasizing your discipline as a part-time worker. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing that key factor head-on, and with your Black male perspective bringing unique resilience to law school challenges, you're well-positioned to turn this around. Keep pushing—admissions like this are just one data point in your journey.
